content.problemStatement:
Alok-nath is man of equality. He needs your help to divide his “sanskars” evenly amongst all his followers. By doing this, Alok-nath can create equality amongst his followers and he'll be called a true “sanskari”.

Alok-nath has **N** sanskars, and **K** followers. Each sanskar is given a numerical value which shows its intensity.

Your task is to determine whether it is possible to allocate all the sanskars to followers in such a way that the sum of intensities of the sanskars allocated to each follower is equal. **Note : **A sanskar can be allocated to only one of the followers.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, denoting the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. The first line of each case contains two integers N and K, with N denoting the number of sanskars and K denoting the number of followers. In the next line are N space separated integers denoting the intensities of each sanskar.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output 'yes' if it is possible to divide his sanskars equally amongst his followers; otherwise output 'no'.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first case, sanskars can be allocated as follows, each follower receiving a total intensity of 6: {1,5}, {2,4}, {6}. In the second case, it is not possible to allocate the sanskars such that each follower receives the same total intensity.
It is not possible to allocate the sanskars such that each follower receives the same total intensity.
The sanskars can be allocated as follows, each follower receiving a total intensity of 1: {1}, {1}.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using a backtracking approach. We try to assign each sanskar to a follower and check if the sum of intensities of the sanskars allocated to each follower is equal. If it is not possible to assign a sanskar to any follower, we backtrack and try a different assignment.
solution.timeComplexity: O(K^N) per test case, O(T*K^N) total. The time complexity is exponential because in the worst case, we have to try all possible assignments of sanskars to followers.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the space complexity is linear because we need to store the intensities of the sanskars and the sums of the intensities allocated to each follower.
